data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "A rapid onset aridification event (300-year drought) reduced agricultural surplus below the threshold required to sustain the specialized bureaucratic class, forcing a devolution to subsistence farming.", "mechanism": "Climate-driven Malthusian collapse of the redistributive surplus.", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite Anatolia", "Levantine city-states", "Mycenaean Greece"], "excludes": ["Nile Delta regions of Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimatic data from speleothems or pollen cores showing stable precipitation levels in Anatolia between 1250-1100 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Explains why Egypt survived (Nile flood independence) and why the collapse was regional yet simultaneous; shifts focus from human agency to environmental determinism."}
